content:
# Disagreement Log — Joshua 17
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA

Joshua 17 covers Manasseh's western allotment, the precedent of Zelophehad's daughters, the interleaved Ephraim-Manasseh boundaries, the failure to dispossess the Canaanites, and the house of Joseph's complaint to Joshua. The boundary descriptions are largely technical and generated few disagreements; the narrative sections (vv. 1-6, 12-18) produced more variation among the translators.

---

## 1. Proper Name Forms: Anglicized vs. Transliterated (throughout)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | Menasheh, Yoseph, Yehoshua, Yarden, Ephrayim, Yissakhar, Yizreel, Makhir, Tzelophchad, Elazar, Moshe, Kenaanite, etc. | Full Hebrew transliteration |
| LOGOS | Manasseh, Joseph, Joshua, Jordan, Ephraim, Issachar, Jezreel, Machir, Zelophehad, Eleazar, Moses, Canaanite, etc. | Standard anglicized forms |
| PNEUMA | Manasseh, Joseph, Joshua, Jordan, Ephraim, Issachar, Jezreel, Machir, Zelophehad, Eleazar, Moses, Canaanite, etc. | Standard anglicized forms |
| **SOPHIA** | **Anglicized forms — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | These names have universally established English equivalents. Unlike YHWH, which carries irreducible theological weight, proper nouns like Joshua, Joseph, and Manasseh do not lose meaning in their anglicized forms. Consistent with SOPHIA convention throughout the project. |

## 2. "Drew Near" vs. "Came Near" vs. "Came Before" (v. 4)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they drew near before Eleazar the priest" | vatiqravna from qarav = drew near |
| LOGOS | "they came near before Eleazar the priest" | Same root, different English |
| PNEUMA | "They came before Eleazar the priest" | Simplified |
| **SOPHIA** | **"they drew near before Eleazar the priest" — EMET upheld** | The Hebrew qarav means "to draw near, approach" and carries formal, even cultic, weight. "Drew near" preserves the deliberateness of the daughters' approach to the judicial assembly. "Came near" (LOGOS) is acceptable but slightly weaker. "Came before" (PNEUMA) loses the spatial movement entirely. |

## 3. "According to the Mouth of YHWH" vs. "In Keeping with YHWH's Command" (v. 4)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "according to the mouth of YHWH" | el pi YHWH — literal |
| LOGOS | "according to the mouth of YHWH" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "in keeping with YHWH's command" | Dynamic equivalent |
| **SOPHIA** | **"according to the mouth of YHWH" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The Hebrew peh (mouth) is an embodied speech metaphor. The inheritance was granted by YHWH's direct spoken word to Moses (Num 27:6-7). "Command" (PNEUMA) is accurate but abstracts the concrete image. |

## 4. "Among Our Brothers" vs. "Among Our Kinsmen" (v. 4)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the midst of our brothers" | betokh acheinu |
| LOGOS | "among our brothers" | Same meaning |
| PNEUMA | "among our kinsmen" | Broader term |
| **SOPHIA** | **"among our brothers" — LOGOS upheld** | ach means "brother." The daughters deliberately use fraternal language to assert their equal standing within the tribe. "Kinsmen" (PNEUMA) is softer and loses the pointed claim. "In the midst of" (EMET) is more literal for betokh but reads awkwardly in English here; "among" (LOGOS) captures the same spatial sense. |

## 5. "Inherited an Inheritance" vs. "Received an Inheritance" (v. 6)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the daughters of Manasseh inherited an inheritance" | Cognate accusative: nachalah...nachal |
| LOGOS | "the daughters of Manasseh received an inheritance" | Natural English |
| PNEUMA | "the daughters of Manasseh received an inheritance" | Natural English |
| **SOPHIA** | **"received an inheritance" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | While the Hebrew uses a cognate accusative (nachal nachalah), "inherited an inheritance" is redundant in English. Unlike the infinitive absolute (which project convention preserves), the cognate accusative is not always best served by literal doubling. "Received" captures the sense without awkwardness. |

## 6. "Upon the Face of Shechem" vs. "Before Shechem" vs. "Which Faces Shechem" (v. 7)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "which is upon the face of Shechem" | al penei Shekhem — literal |
| LOGOS | "which is before Shechem" | Standard English for al penei |
| PNEUMA | "which faces Shechem" | Dynamic |
| **SOPHIA** | **"which is before Shechem" — LOGOS upheld** | al penei is a spatial preposition meaning "in front of, before, facing." "Upon the face of" (EMET) is overliteral. "Before" (LOGOS) preserves the directional sense in natural English. "Faces" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but makes the landmark the grammatical subject of an active verb, which slightly reshapes the Hebrew syntax. |

## 7. "To the Right" vs. "Southward" (v. 7)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the border went to the right" | ha-yamin = right |
| LOGOS | "the border went to the right" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the boundary went southward" | Interprets yamin as south |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to the right" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The Hebrew yamin means "right hand," and by convention denotes "south" when facing east. Preserving "to the right" retains the Hebrew directional idiom. "Southward" (PNEUMA) is correct interpretation but flattens the cultural image. |

## 8. "Daughter-Towns" vs. "Daughters" vs. "Surrounding Towns" (vv. 11, 16)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "its daughters" | benoteha — literal |
| LOGOS | "its daughter-towns" | Preserves metaphor + clarifies |
| PNEUMA | "its surrounding towns" | Meaning only |
| **SOPHIA** | **"daughter-towns" — LOGOS upheld** | The Hebrew benoteha (lit. "her daughters") uses kinship language for satellite settlements. "Daughters" (EMET) is literal but opaque to English readers. "Surrounding towns" (PNEUMA) loses the kinship metaphor entirely. "Daughter-towns" (LOGOS) is the best synthesis: the Hebrew image is preserved while the meaning is transparent. |

## 9. "The Three of the Heights" vs. "Three of the Height" vs. "The Three of Naphath" (v. 11)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "three of the height" | hannafet as common noun (singular) |
| LOGOS | "the three of the heights" | hannafet as common noun (plural) |
| PNEUMA | "the three of Naphath" | hannafet as proper noun |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the three of the heights" — LOGOS upheld** | The Hebrew sheloshet hannafet is genuinely ambiguous. The LXX Vaticanus treats it as a proper noun (Napheth). However, the most probable reading is that nafet/nafah means "height, ridge" — a common noun describing elevated districts. "The heights" (LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew structure while conveying the likely meaning. PNEUMA's proper-noun approach is defensible but less informative. |

## 10. "Was Determined" vs. "Persisted" vs. "Were Determined" (v. 12)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Canaanite was determined to dwell" | Singular collective + ho'il |
| LOGOS | "the Canaanites persisted in dwelling" | Plural + "persisted" |
| PNEUMA | "the Canaanites were determined to stay" | Plural + "determined" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the Canaanites were determined to dwell" — Blend** | SOPHIA uses the plural "Canaanites" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for natural English, with "determined" (EMET/PNEUMA) for the Hiphil of ya'al, which conveys willful resolve — stronger than "persisted" (LOGOS). "Dwell" (EMET/LOGOS) is retained over "stay" (PNEUMA) as the standard rendering of yashav. |

## 11. Infinitive Absolute: "Dispossessing, They Did Not Dispossess" vs. "Utterly Dispossess" vs. "Drive Out Completely" (v. 13)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "dispossessing they did not dispossess him" | Infinitive absolute preserved with doubling |
| LOGOS | "they did not utterly dispossess them" | Adverbial rendering |
| PNEUMA | "they did not drive them out completely" | Dynamic paraphrase |
| **SOPHIA** | **"dispossessing, they did not dispossess them" — EMET upheld (with plural adjustment)** | Per project convention, infinitive absolute constructions are rendered with doubling to preserve the Hebrew rhetorical intensification. EMET's rendering most faithfully captures the horesh lo horish construction. The pronoun is adjusted to plural "them" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for natural English. |

## 12. "A Great People" vs. "A Numerous People" (vv. 14-15, 17)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a great people" | am rav |
| LOGOS | "a numerous people" | rav = numerous |
| PNEUMA | "a numerous people" | rav = numerous |
| **SOPHIA** | **"a numerous people" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | The Hebrew rav in this context is about population size, not greatness in the abstract. The sons of Joseph are arguing they deserve more land because they are too many for one allotment. "Numerous" captures the quantitative emphasis. In v. 17, Joshua affirms both their numbers ("a numerous people") and their strength ("great is your strength") — using "great" for both would blur the distinction. |

## 13. "Too Narrow" vs. "Too Cramped" (v. 15)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "too narrow for you" | ats = narrow, constricted |
| LOGOS | "too narrow for you" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "too cramped for you" | Dynamic equivalent |
| **SOPHIA** | **"too narrow for you" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The Hebrew ats means "narrow, constricted." "Narrow" is the direct English equivalent. "Cramped" (PNEUMA) is close but adds a connotation of discomfort rather than spatial limitation. |

## 14. "Is Not Found Sufficient" vs. "Is Not Enough" (v. 16)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The hill country is not found [sufficient] for us" | lo yimmatse' — literal |
| LOGOS | "The hill country is not enough for us" | Idiomatic English |
| PNEUMA | "The hill country is not enough for us" | Same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"is not enough for us" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | The Hebrew lo yimmatse' lanu literally means "will not be found for us" — an idiom for insufficiency. "Is not enough" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) conveys the meaning clearly. EMET's literal rendering requires brackets to be comprehensible, signaling that the idiom does not transfer directly. |

## 15. "Its Goings Out" vs. "Its Farthest Extents" vs. "Its Farthest Reaches" (v. 18)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "its goings out shall be yours" | totse'otav — literal |
| LOGOS | "its farthest extents shall be yours" | Interpretive |
| PNEUMA | "its farthest reaches will be yours" | Dynamic |
| **SOPHIA** | **"its farthest extents shall be yours" — LOGOS upheld** | The Hebrew totse'ot (lit. "goings-out") refers to the outermost edges of a territory — where a boundary "goes out" to its end. "Goings out" (EMET) is opaque in English. "Farthest extents" (LOGOS) and "farthest reaches" (PNEUMA) both capture the meaning well; "extents" is slightly more precise as a geographical term. |

## 16. Narrative Connector wayehi: "And It Was" (v. 13)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And it was, when the sons of Israel grew strong" | wayehi preserved |
| LOGOS | "And it was, when the Sons of Israel grew strong" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "When the Sons of Israel grew stronger" | wayehi dropped |
| **SOPHIA** | **"And it was, when the Sons of Israel grew strong" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The wayehi construction is a structural marker of Hebrew narrative, signaling a temporal shift. SOPHIA preserves it throughout, consistent with project convention. |

## 17. "Great Is Your Strength" vs. "You Have Great Power" vs. "With Great Strength" (v. 17)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "great is your strength" | koach gadol — predicate-first |
| LOGOS | "you have great power" | Restructured English |
| PNEUMA | "with great strength" | Prepositional |
| **SOPHIA** | **"great is your strength" — EMET upheld** | The Hebrew places the adjective gadol before the noun koach in a declarative predicate construction. "Great is your strength" preserves this word order and creates a dignified, oracular tone appropriate to Joshua's formal address. "You have great power" (LOGOS) is accurate but loses the rhetorical weight. "With great strength" (PNEUMA) turns it into a prepositional modifier rather than an independent declaration. |

---
